processing_notes: Strip spikes from stems when light tan and dry. Easily winnowed.
Cut mature seed heads when dry. Rub through fine screens to separate tiny black shiny seeds from chaff.
Propagation is possible by scarified seed and softwood or hardwood cuttings.
Panicles dry and turn tan. Cut the narrow, contracted panicles directly from the stem.
Burrs attach to clothing/gloves; wear heavy leather gloves. Extract hooked burrs when dry and brown.
Strip dry seed heads. Triple-awned seeds are sharp and can penetrate skin/clothing; handle with gloves.
Strip reddish-purple awned seeds when dry and easily detached. Handle carefully due to sharp awns.
Harvest flowering stalks when they dry out and seeds become dark. Crush and winnow gently.
Harvest pods just as they begin to split. Wear gloves (sap is an irritant). Shake in a bag or strip fluff from seed.
General collection for Milkvetch: Gather inflated or woody pods when dry and rattling. Note: Many species are highly toxic.
Collect tiny, lightly hairy pods when turn papery and tan. Handle gently.
Collect pods when thick, woody, and purplish-brown. Break open tough pod walls to release seeds.
Collect pods when thick, woody, and purplish-brown. Break open tough pod walls to release seeds.
Long narrow cylindrical pods. Collect when brown and crisp; seeds drop rapidly once pods split.
TOXIC. Pods swollen, inflated, papery, and mottled with purple. Pop open papery walls to extract seed.
Pendulous, smooth, paper-thin pods. Harvest when light tan. Crush lightly or shake out.
Woody, densely hairy, oblong pods. Rub tough pods against wire screen to liberate seeds.
TOXIC. Hard, curved, smooth brown seed pods hidden under woolly foliage. Wear gloves to strip and crack open.
Collect small, slightly curved pods when dry and tan. Shake or press to separate seed.
TOXIC (Selenium). Noticeable sulfur odor. Large, woody, inflated oblong pods. Collect when dry and straw-colored.
TOXIC (Selenium). Noticeable sulfur odor. Large, woody, inflated oblong pods. Collect when dry and straw-colored.
TOXIC. Large, balloon-like transparent inflated pods. Pops effortlessly between fingers to release seeds.
Strip hardened, silvery-grey wedge-shaped fruiting bracts directly from branches.
Strip the papery, four-winged fruits from branches when dry and straw-colored. Highly durable seed.
Strip the small, wedge-shaped bracteoles when brown. Wear gloves due to spiny branches.
Strip mature, curved 'eyelash' seed heads by hand pulling upward on the stalk. Cure in paper bags.
Strip nodding, golden-brown panicles by hand or clip stalks. Easily cleaned by light winnowing.
Hand-strip or clip dry panicles when tan. Winnow to remove chaff.
Collect the hard burrs located close to the ground using specialized equipment or careful hand-raking.
Harvest the circular, flat schizocarps once they turn crisp and dark brown. Rub to separate segments.
Clip dried cylindrical woody capsules when brown and split at the tip. Shake out tiny seeds.
Collect dried papery capsules after purple flowers fade. Tip over and shake tiny seeds into a bag.
Gather nodding brown capsules when dry and nodding pores open. Shake gently over paper.
Strip clustered perigynia (seed sacs) when golden-brown and easily pulled from stems.
Clip dry, brittle brown seed capsules. Shake over a paper bag; seeds are extremely tiny and dust-like.
Collect fluffy, dry seed heads after tiny daisy flowers fade. Store in dry paper bags.
Gather yellowish-white berries when mature. Mash and wash away pulp to separate small seeds.
Collect tiny three-lobed capsules when dry. Wear gloves as milky latex sap can irritate skin.
Gather dried capsules. Crush gently over a fine mesh screen; avoid eye/skin contact with sap.
Collect dry capsules. Crush and winnow gently through fine screens.
Hand-strip feathery seed spikes pulling upward along the stem when tan.
Strip curved, segmented pods when brittle and brown. Break pods apart across screens.
Harvest fluffy, wind-dispersed seed heads in late autumn when dry and easily loosened from twigs.
Pluck fluffy seed heads when dry. Heavy leather gloves required due to extremely sharp spines.
Collect large, inflated, papery pods when papery and rattling. Break open pods to release hard seeds.
Non-photosynthetic parasitic plant on oak roots. Collect dry, woody cone-like stalks and shake out seeds.
Gather rough, dry nutlet clusters when stalks turn grey-brown. Caution: stiff bristly plant hairs can irritate skin.
Strip dense, silky-hairy seed heads when brown. Rub heads across wire mesh to loosen hulls.
Strip mature spikes when they turn dark brown. Rub spikes vigorously to free individual small seeds.
Strip cylinder spikes when rough and grey-brown. Rub across screens to remove hulls.
Clip delicate, open panicles when straw-colored. Light winnowing removes delicate chaff.
Clip stalks bearing narrow siliques (pods) when tan. Crush pods over a fine screen to collect tiny orange-brown seeds.
Harvest intact heads before they disintegrate into the wind. Sharp awns; wear leather gloves.
Hand-strip erect seed heads when golden-tan. Clean via rubbing and winnowing.
Strip slender, erect seed spikes when tan/brown. Easily winnowed.
Strip open, airy panicles when dry. Seeds are very small and reddish-brown; sift through fine mesh.
Collect stork's-bill seed pods just as they darken and spring open into coiled, corkscrew awns.
Gather long, slender pods when dry and brittle. Crush open and sift out small, oblong seeds.
Clip four-angled dry pods when straw-colored. Crush gently and screen out chaff.
Clip dry, slender siliques. Crush and winnow through mesh screens.
Collect three-chambered capsules when brown. Caution: milky sap causes irritation.
Same as Krascheninnikovia ceratoides; strip dense white-hairy seed clusters when fluffy.
Gather small, round capsules after blue flowers drop. Crush open to extract tiny seeds.
Hand-strip narrow panicles when golden-tan and seeds detach with light tension.
Gather round, dry seed heads once flower rays fall and centers turn dark brown. Rub to separate seeds.
Collect small, woody, diamond-shaped nutlets along stalks when they turn brown and drop easily.
Clip dried flower heads when brown. Rub heads over a screen to separate small nutlets.
Collect sticky, resinous button-like flower heads when brown and dry. Rub across screens to extract seeds.
Harvest dry aromatic stalks. Crush lightly over a box or sheet to release tiny black egg-shaped seeds.
Harvest heads when backs turn yellow-brown. Rub heads across mesh to detach seeds. Watch for weevils.
Pluck fluffy seed heads. Wear leather gloves; leaves feature rigid, spine-tipped teeth.
Collect the sharp-pointed seeds with very long, feathery awns. Store dry; awns drill into clothes/skin.
Gather fluffy, parachute-topped seed heads when tan and easily detached.
Strip the whitish/straw-colored spikelets from the stem when they detach easily with light finger pressure.
Strip brittle seed heads when dry. Sharp awns can work into skin/fabrics; wear gloves.
Pluck dry, wedge-shaped seeds with papery scales from flat flower heads when tan.
Collect papery capsules when dark brown and cracking open. Shake out black, wedge-shaped seeds.
Clip dried cylindrical capsules when light brown. Tip over and tap out small seeds.
Strip dark brown seed capsules from rush stems. Crush pods over paper to release dust-like seeds.
Strip golden-brown capsules along stem tips. Rub open capsules to harvest tiny seeds.
Gather dark brown, clustered seed heads. Crush and sift through fine tea-strainer mesh.
Strip mature capsules when tan/brown. Seeds are extremely minute.
Pick plump, blue-glaucous fleshy cones ('berries'). Extract the single seed by mashing and washing pulp.
Gently strip the cottony, woolly white seed clusters off branches. Leave plenty on the plant for wildlife.
Collect burr-like nutlets when brown. Stickseed burrs catch on clothing; rub through screens with gloved hands.
Strip stalks bearing flattened, round silicles (pods) when straw-colored. Rub to release small reddish seeds.
Strip dense racemes of flat disc-like pods when dry and tan. Winnow away papery pod hulls.
Hand-strip dried seed stalks. Crush flat pods and screen out chaff.
Gather dried stems with mature flat pods. Rub vigorously and winnow.
Pluck fluffy pappus-capped seed heads when dry and easily airborne. Store in cool, dry breathable paper bags.
Clip oval capsules when brown and splitting at the top. Shake out winged, disc-like seeds.
Collect slender pods when dark brown before they twist open explosively to disperse seeds.
Gather ripe, plump, bright red or reddish-purple berries. Clean by gently washing away pulp to extract small seeds, or dry the whole berries for storage.
Pluck bright red/purplish fleshy fruits when ripe. Mash and wash away fruit pulp to collect dark, pitted seeds. Handle cactus spines with forceps.
Gather coiled, black, ripe seed pods. Rub pods across textured rubber or screens to extract seeds.
Collect spiny, coiled burrs when brown and dry.
Collect coiled, spiral pods when dark brown and brittle. Crush pods and winnow.
Regularly check and pick cup-like seed heads when they turn hard and dark grey. Seeds drop immediately when ripe.
Collect translucent, twin-sphered capsules just as they turn straw-colored and split horizontally.
Collect heavily spined, ribbed pods when dark brown. Heavy leather gloves essential.
Collect hard, ribbed, oblong fruits (anthocarps) when they turn grey-brown and drop easily from bracts.
Non-vascular bryophyte (moss). Reproduces by spores produced in capsules (sporophytes), not seeds. Keep damp if propagating patches.
Hand-strip slender, purplish-tan seed heads pulling upward when seeds detach easily.
Strip compact, narrow panicles when tan and ripe. Winnow gently.
Strip delicate, open, cobwebby panicles when dry and purplish-brown.
Clip long cylindrical seed pods once they turn woody and brown. Shake or crush to release small seeds.
Twist off ripe red/purplish fleshy fruits using tongs. Mash pulp in water; viable hard seeds sink while pulp floats. Beware of glochids (tiny spines).
Same as species; harvest ripe red fruit with tongs, wash away pulp to save hard disc-like seeds.
Collect juicy red/purple tunas (fruits) with tongs. Mash and rinse thoroughly in colander to extract hard tan seeds.
Gather dry or spiny fruits using tongs. Extract hard, thick-rimmed seeds by crushing or washing out pulp.
Parasitic plant lacking chlorophyll. Collect dried flowering stalks when brown; tap out microscopic dust-like seeds.
Capsules explode when touched at maturity. Enclose ripening capsules in fine mesh bags or harvest just before turning crisp.
Pluck white, fluffy, dandelion-like seed heads when dry and ready to float on wind.
Strip plump, rounded, bead-like seeds from narrow panicles when they turn brownish-green and firm.
Hand-strip or cut erect seed spikes when they match a uniform tan/straw color. Easy to clean via winnowing.
CAUTION: Sap combined with sunlight causes severe chemical burns (phytophotodermatitis). Wear long sleeves/gloves. Collect flat, winged seeds when brown.
Collect tiny white capsules before they split. Dry in a closed paper bag as seeds are ejected forcefully.
Harvest two-valved woody capsules after they turn dark brown and crack open. Invert and shake out seeds.
Collect erect stalks bearing dry, brittle brown capsules. Crush capsules gently or shake to harvest.
Gather small papery capsules when straw-colored after pink flowers fade. Shake out tiny black seeds.
Collect papery capsules when dry and light brown before they split. Enclose in paper bags.
Collect papery, lantern-like husks when brown and berries inside turn yellow/tan. Mash berries in water to clean seeds.
Gather the tiny papery round spheres when they rattle and turn translucent tan. Crush open to extract seeds.
Collect dark, resinous cones just as scales begin to open. Dry cones in a warm room until scales open fully, then shake out winged seeds.
Strip erect, cylindrical seed heads when dark brown and dry. Rub across screens to separate small brown seeds.
Pluck large, straw-colored, basket-like heads when dry. Break open heads to collect plump seeds with stiff crowns.
Hand-strip open pyramid-shaped panicles when golden-brown.
Pick soft, ripe red/yellow wild plums. Remove flesh immediately by hand-mashing or soaking to save the large pit.
Gather dried, persistent yellow flower heads when they turn papery and tan. Rub gently to detach seeds.
Collect papery flower heads when dry and brittle. Rub across mesh screen to separate achenes.
Strip open, spreading panicles when straw-colored and seeds detach easily.
Collect mature acorns directly off branches or fresh ground drops. Discard any with tiny exit holes (weevils).
Pull off elongated grey-brown cones when dry and rough. Crumbles effortlessly in fingers with an anise scent.
Pull off short, cylindrical grey-brown cones when rough and dry. Rub between fingers to liberate seeds.
Strip rough, rounded seed heads when dry and brown. Rub open rough hulls.
Pluck feathery, dandelion-like seed puffs when fully open and dry.
Collect leathery, constricted pods when dark brown and dry. Break pods open to extract hard, yellowish seeds.
Collect segmented capsule clusters once they turn dry and brown. Caution: fine plant hairs can cause skin itching.
Collect inflated, bladder-like papery pods when dry and rattling. Crush pods to release small seeds.
Clip diffuse, airy panicles when seeds turn a firm amber-brown. Run stems through hands to pop out small seeds.
Cut or strip seed stalks once tiny grain-like seeds darken. Often remains partly bound in a sheath.
Pluck dry, silvery-hairy seed heads when disc turns brown and achenes loosen easily.
Pick dark brown, disc-shaped heads after rays fade. Winnow outer papery bracts away from slender seeds.
Pick dry, brown disc heads after yellow petals fade. Winnow away the papery outer chaff scales.
Collect dry, brown flower heads. Rub heads across textured screens to free small kidney-shaped seeds.
Collect dry, reddish-brown flower heads when crisp. Rub across mesh screens to extract seeds.
Collect entire dried flower heads when the small yellow rays fade to a brittle paper-bag appearance.Populus fremontii
column11: Collect green capsules from female trees just as they begin to split and show white cotton. Dry in a closed screen or paper bag so wind doesn't blow cotton away. Rub cotton over a fine mesh screen or vacuum-screen to separate tiny seeds. Seeds have very short viability (days to weeks); plant immediately or store frozen.

processing_notes: Same as Fremont's: harvest opening cottony pods from female trees. Separate minute seeds from lint via rubbing across fine screens. Sow immediately on moist substrate.

Collect mature brown cones just as scales begin to open, or shake open cones to collect fallen pine nuts. Highly resinous sap—wear disposable or heavy leather gloves. Store nuts in a cool, dry area or freeze to prevent oils from rancidifying.

Collect yellowish capsules just as they crack open to release cottony seed fluff. Clean by rubbing gently over screens. Seed viability is extremely short (often under 2 weeks); sow immediately on wet soil or propagate via dormant hardwood cuttings.
Hand-pick reddish-brown to orange drupes when firm and mature. Pulp is thin and sweet/dry; rub berries across mesh with water to clean out hard, pitted stones.

Pluck fluffy, pinkish-white feathery seed heads (achenes with feathery styles) directly from shrubs when dry and easily detached. Light winnowing removes leftover floral debris. Excellent native shrub for erosion control and arroyo stabilization.

Harvest dense, fluffy, wind-dispersed seed heads late in autumn/winter when fully dry and easily loosened from twigs. Rub gently across mesh screens to loosen pappus bristles from achenes. Prolific pioneer species on disturbed soil.

CAUTION: Entire plant contains toxic alkaloids (solanine), and stems feature prickly spines—wear heavy gloves. Harvest yellow to orange-brown berries once they become wrinkled and dry/leathery. Mash and wash away pulp in water to isolate small brown seeds.

Collect flat, reddish-brown, bristly/hairy seed pods when dry and brittle. CAUTION: Pods and branches feature sharp prickles and bristles—wear heavy leather gloves. Crush pods across wire screens to free the hard kidney-shaped seeds. Like many legumes, seeds have an extremely hard, water-impermeable seed coat; they require hot water soaking or mechanical scarification before planting to break dormancy.
Formally classified as Gaura parviflora/mollis. Hand-strip tall, velvety flowering stalks when the small, spindle-shaped woody nutlets turn dark brown and detach easily. Winnow away leaf fragments.
